summary: Gruber examines the economics and realities of being an independent Mac developer, using Brent Simmons's sale of NetNewsWire to NewsGator as a case study. He argues that the romantic notion of 'The Life' — independently selling software and coding full-time — breaks down at scale because support costs grow with every sale. Simmons was so successful that he could no longer spend his time writing code, instead drowning in customer support. The NewsGator acquisition, rather than being a sellout, actually gets Simmons closer to The Life by freeing him to focus on programming while NewsGator handles the business overhead. Gruber supports this with detailed RSS feed statistics showing NetNewsWire's dominant market position among Mac users.
key_points: Software sales follow a power-law distribution, but unlike books, each sale brings incremental support costs that consume development time
The indie developer paradox: by the time you sell enough licenses to quit your day job, you have so many users that you spend most of your time on support instead of coding
NetNewsWire held a staggering 51-74% share of RSS aggregator usage across Daring Fireball's various feeds, demonstrating massive market dominance
Simmons's wife Sheila was the 'secret ingredient' handling support and business operations — without her, Ranchero couldn't have lasted as long as it did
The NewsGator acquisition paradoxically moved Simmons closer to 'The Life' than independence did, by letting him focus on code while a company handles the rest
money_quotes: Once a developer gets enough paying users to consider quitting his day job so he can devote full-time effort to writing code, he's quite possibly got so many paying users that he'll spend much of his time helping customers in ways other than writing code.
Novelists aren't expected to help people read their books; musicians don't help fans play their CDs; but programmers are expected to help you with their software.
The whole point of The Life is the idea that you can just sit around all day hacking on code and watch the money roll in through Kagi or eSellerate.
By selling his company and working on NetNewsWire as a NewsGator employee, Brent has actually gotten closer to The Life than he was as an indie.
What it really boils down to is a crackerjack programmer carving out a space where he can continue focusing on one of the best apps the Mac has ever seen.
NetNewsWire's market share among Daring Fireball members is simply titanic.
People who are willing to pay money to support Daring Fireball are more likely than average to be willing to pay money to purchase software.
key_insight: The romantic ideal of the solo indie developer breaks down at scale because success creates a support burden that prevents the very coding work that made the product successful in the first place.
